name: 1° AV Block - First-Degree AV Block
identifyBy: PR interval >200ms (>5 small squares) in every beat
Every P wave is followed by a QRS (1:1 conduction)
Constant PR interval - does not vary

measurements.pWaves: Normal - every P followed by QRS

management: First degree AV block alone requires no acute treatment
Identify cause: medications (digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers), inferior MI, myocarditis, Lyme disease
Monitor - may progress to higher-degree block in acute inferior MI
Stop causative drugs if symptomatic or PR >300ms
Pacing not indicated for 1° AV block alone
pearls: 1° AV block is not a 'block' in the true sense - all impulses conduct, just slowly
PR >300ms: very long - P wave may merge with preceding T wave; may cause symptoms (pacemaker syndrome physiology)
Lyme carditis: 1° AV block is the most common cardiac manifestation - serological testing warranted
1° AV block can be physiological in athletes due to high vagal tone
Progression to 2° or 3° block: important in inferior MI context - monitor closely
differentials: 2° AV block Mobitz I (PR lengthens then drops)
